CURRENT AFFAIRS,

The Church of England’s Faithful Garrison in Ire-
land were in Conference in “London’’ (derry) last
week. Many Union Jacks were, naturally, flapping in
the brecze. The entrance of the Protestant Primate in
company with the Duke of Abercorn, Grand Master of
the pernicious secret society by the name of the Free
and Accepted Masons of Ireland, and the Protestant
Archbishop of Dublin was, according to the report in
Alf Foz, “the signal for a loud burst of applause.”
And the libeller tells us that the applause “was re-
newed immediately afterwards on the entry of the
Bishop of Derry.”” The Protestant Bishop of Derry, we
may remind our readers, is one of the “Grand Chap-
lains ”’ of the secret society by the name of the Orange
Society. Amongst those present were the Protestant
Bishop of Ossory, another “ Grand Chaplain ”* to the
Orange bolt-throwers, the Protestant Dean of Raphoe,
a “ Deputy Grand Chaplain *’ of the Orange Lambs, and
the Protestant Bishop of Down one of the ‘Grand

Chaplains ** to the Freomasons of Ireland. Oh, we are
sure it was a “tolerant ”’ party. The Protestant Bishop
of Derry, one of the “ Grand ‘Chaplains ”’ of the Orange
Bolt-throwers, presided and delivered the Presidential
address. This “Grand Chaplain”’ of the blind, ig-
norant Orangemen dropped into paradox. He said :—
‘,‘ As we thus exchange hopes, opinions, and desires, it
is inevitable that we should be conscious, first of all,
of our position as a National Church in the midst of a
population which is not ours.”” Certainly “a National
Church in the midst of a population which is not ours,”
is not bad from a spokesman of the Plantation of Ire-
land. Bernard Shaw and wits of that ilk had better
look to their laurels. “By us,” exclaimed this exotic
Cleric, “ By us all over Ireland He sustains a Church
which is not Italianized, a creed which is not super-
stitious, an order which is not slavish, a laity who are
not gagged, a patriotism whose hands are not red with
Irish blood, a concern for learning which has not
through fifty barren years found or invented excuses
for denouncing every effort to enlighten the Irish
people.  (Applause.)’” Well said, great “ Grand Chap-
lain *” of the Orange Bolt-throwers. thy concern for
learning may well be symbolised by a Trinity branded
herring! Alf Fox is surely a “heretic’’ now, for that
great organ of “a concern for learning’’ recently re-
marked in commenting on the exhibitions of the Modern
Literary Course, Junior Grade, “thirty-seven Exhibi-
tions were given, of which no less than thirty-six have
gone to Roman Catholic schools. This result is a very
surprising one, and will, no doubt, give rise to con-
siderable searchings of heart in the Protestant
schools.”” But perhaps there is no room for such dis-
agreeable things as “ searchings of heart ’’ in the philo-
sophy of a “ Grand Chaplain ’’ of the Bolt-throwers of
Sandy Row. A demagogue like the Protestant Bishop
of London (derry) is none of your Hamlet-like wobblers
of the “ Zrush ’’ T'imes type. Verily, there is no bigot
like an old bigot. This “ Grand Chaplain >’ of Orange-
men further on dropped neck deep into unconscious
comicality. He said:—“In Ireland the cradle of
national education was in Kildare place, and the Church
was tho nwrse who rocked it. (Hear, hear.)” The
Church of England’s Faithful Garrison in Ireland rock-
ing national education in Kildare Place certainly adds
much to the gaiety of the nation, and we are laughing
as we make record of the comic picture.

The Protestant Bishop of Ossory, one of the “ Grand
Chaplains ’’ of the Orangemen, in the course of a paper,
as reported in ZEngland’s Faithful Garrison Times
said:—“The fact that the Church of Ireland is the
Church of a small minority in this land is not one that
we are likely to forget.”” Why carry on the farce then
—if it be not far worse than a farce—of misnaming
their exotic sect, “The Church of Ireland?’ They
know as well as we do that it is merely a sect of the
many sects into which England’s faithful garrison in
this country is divided and sub-divided.

The blindness that doth hedge the mind of a cast-iron
bigot is intense.  The Protestant Bishop of Ossory
said:—“But it is pre-eminently the duty of a Na-
tional Church to be over independent of local passions,
of professional politics, and of the temptation to follow
the-lead of interested demagogues.”” And the exotic
clergyman who uttered that is a “ Grand Chaplain ** of
the ignorant and rather barbarous Orangemen !

All that the “tame’ Papists get for much of their
“ tolerance proving ” is that the results of it are flung
insultingly in their faces. One of the speakers at this
Conference of an exotic Church said:—There was

THE T.EADER.

Scarcely a country town throughout the length and
braadth of the land in which they did not find that the
most successful business men belonged to their com-
m’l}}non.” This speaker keeps up his heart by faith in
a “no far distant date.’” He said:—“ A day would
come when there would be a revolt from tho spiritual
tyranny of Romanism, and with it would come ‘the

opportunity of their Church for which they should be
prepared.’’

Ardilaun’s paper, the Daily Eapress, climbed into
the giddy heights of raimeis over the harangue of the
Protestant Bishop of London(derry). The Kapress re-
marked :—“ The President, Dr. Chadwick, had pre-
pared, as might be expected, a brilliant and incisive
a(_ldress, as he welcomed Churchmen to the Maiden
City, pregnant with memories of heroic struggle and
hard-fought liberty, and hallowed by the memory of a
siege whose record is a glory to our native land.””  This
is not bad from a paper run by a titled Bung.

England’s Faithful Garvison ‘is getting afraid of the
Trish Revival; they are beginning to wonder if it will
pay to keep altogether out of it. The Protestant Bishop
of Clogher said :—* He was not a member of the Gaelic
League—(laughter)—and he did not see his way to be-
come one, but he thought they should be careful in their
treatment of the enthusiastic movement that was being
made to revive that language. He did not mean to
say that the movement was wise from every point of
view. For instance it had become identified with a
particular religious body, but they ought to think
whether they could not by acting wisely in this matter
impart to that movement a greater degree of wisdom
and moderation.  If they could not join the Gaelic
League, they might consider whether they could not
have a Gaelic League of their own. They
might at least take an interest in the Irish
language, and let it be known that they were not out
of sympathy with the Irish language and Irish history.
_(Applause.)” The Protestant Bishop of Clogher was, as
it were, feeling his way. The Irish Language Move-
ment is getting a little too masculine now to be ignored
by England’s Faithful Garrison, even though Irish was
voted “ uncommercial ’ by the wise Rathmines Commis-
sioners. The Protestant Bishop throws out the sug-
gestion that the members of his sect should consider
the advisability of starting a sectarian Gaelic League
of their own. We have nothing to say against the
suggestion, and we would welcome the formation of an
Episcopalian Gaelic League in view of the fact that
such persons as the Protestant Bishop of Clogher cannot
see their way to join the non-denominational Gaelic
League that at present is spread all over the land.

We note that Dunraven’s “ Reform Association,’’ in
its recent published memorandum, states that—“ A
united people constitutes the only solid foundation on
which the fabric of nationality and the prosperity of a
country can securely rest.”’ Perhaps the Reform Asso-
ciation would stoop to the low level of definitions, and
let us know what is “the fabric of nationality ’’ as
understood by Lord Dunraven and his friends.

‘One would think that the Senate of the Royal Uni-
‘versity of Ireland contained a sufficient number of
“tame’’ Catholics already without adding a fresh and
quite notorious “tame’’ to the list. Already it had
Sir Christopher Nixon, Lord Chief Baron Palles, The
O’Connor Don, Dr. Starkie, Lord Killanin, and others.
Now we see that the place of the late Lord Kenmare is
filled up by no less a person than a man of the “edu-
cated Catholic”’ type of our friend Charles E. Mattin,
D.L.

\

Mr. William O’Brien’s now paper is against tho /'fcc-
man; but we do not notice that it is against Bung.
Perhaps the gentleman who collects some £14,000,000

. yearly from this poor country is not in any degree a na-
fional evil according to William. We courited no less than
twelve Bung advertisements in Williath’s first number.
A Bung ttading as A. Roche and Co., King St., Cork,

OcToBER 7, 1905.

advertises “an Irish speciality,” and this precious
“ speciality "’ is a commodity by the name. of the
“Triple Crown Brand,’”’ verily a sort of Triumvirate in
the Bung line.  Another Bung tells us “The ‘Irish
People’ all say’’ that his whiskey “is the finest and
best in Dublin.”” When the readers of Mr. O’Brien’s
paper have read the twelve Bung advertisements, and
have chosen a few for their patronage, they can turn in
due time to the advertising pages in the first number,
and they will sce close on half a column announce-
ment that may have a particular interest for them.
This is an advertisement of an institute established for
“The Treatmont and Cure of Alcoholic Diseases.”” If
William’s paper gives its readers a wide choice in the
matter of Bung, at least it suggests an institution in
which incbriated readers may look for a cure.

««

Alas for William that life is so short! Irish political
speeches as they are speechified are usually things to be
skipped even when served up condensed on the day after
they were orated to the civilised and uncivilised world.
William gives us No. 1. of a series of suppressed
speeches! The first instalment of this thrilling series
was delivered on August 12th—rather stale now, Wil-
liam—isn’t it?  The speech runs to upwards of six
columns! And we confess we blithely skipped the lot.
There are also six columus of “ Voices of Welcome.”
Evidently anyone who welcomes William is contem-
plated by that gentleman under a powerful microscope.
The world does not know its greatest men, but William
is going to alter all that in his paper, and a great many
of the erstwhile unknown great men of the world are
dragged into light. There are nearly eleven columns of
“ Notes and Comments.”” If we have not read the notes
we have counted them. They number 48, and the name
of the Freceman occurs one or more times in 21 of them!
Is the Simply Deplorable worth all that?

Here are a few phrases culled from No. 1 of “Our
Mission *’— diabolical genius,”” “Mr. Sexton’s sple-
netic energy.”’  One would think that the late Zrish
People was never suppressed by Wyndham for certain
references to the King from the following:—* Behind
them all and above them all, it is a secret of Policinello
that Sir Antony MacDonnell brought over the fair
wishes of a personage whose good offices as a peacemaker
the Republics of France and the United States, no less
than the imperial powers of Germany and even Russia,
do not disdain to court.”  And even though William
followed Lansdowne to Canada, we now read:—"He
(Sir Antony) was the nominee and trusted friend of
Lord Lansdowne, who was very much the most potent
and successful member of the Ministry.”” We take a
few more phrases— Glorious project,” “ triumphant
policy,”” “most puissant combination,” “a wondrous
star of hope,”’ “epoch-making events.”’ Oh, those
epochs! “The wisdom of petty carpers and huxters,
“hunting down like wolves,” “superb and irresistible
inducement,”” “the underground conspiracy,’”’ “ open-
ing gloriously,” “ young landlordism,”” “all parties, all
interests, the stars in their courses,’”” “a torture and a
horror,” “magnificent prospects,” “ruin and bitter-
ness,” ““ an unquenchable fury,”” “loathsome charges,"
“u vile man-hunt.”” You may place all that and more
before us in a paper, William, but, happily, you can’t
‘make us read it. Seriously after five years of the intel-
loctual and thoughtful, LEApER the age of screech has
somewhat abated in Ircland. William would find a
better audience if he whispered for a change.

Wo have before us a mass of documentary evidence
which tolls two interesting stories in which the Great
“Bick ”’ and Sourface Railway play a leading part. A
genblqmnn travelled from England to Cork, and brought
his bicycle with him. The following bxtract from a
lotter to the Great “Sick ’’ and Sourface Railway from
the gentleman whose bicycle was: delayed in transit will
explain itself—it is dated 13th September, and is ad-
dvessed to M. J. H. Bell, who once, it will be remem-
Pm’dz ‘had an historic interview with us in our offices—
“‘Whilst T am obliged for your favour of the 7th inst.
¥e claim, T must say that I think it is absurd to be

g

P

e Ry

mr——— \.’ o

Oc;ronmi 7, 1905.

explained bcf;ora that the North Western ticket was
only to Dublin (my own ticket was to Cork), and there
the North Western delivered my bicycle quite safely. I
procured a ticket from your company at North Wall,
and one o_f your men put the bicycle in the Cork train-

I saw this done as I examined the labels, etc., whilst
the machine was in van. Surely it was not the fauls
of the North Western people; if it was they must have
sent a man round specially to take it out of your frain,
a t]?ll\gl. feel certain they could not possibly have done.
It is quite plain that the fault is on the part of your
company, and I cannot but think it would be much
more honourable and business-like on the part of your
company to admit it, and settle the paltry claim made.
From time to time I notice in the Dublin papers com-
ments on the working of the G. S. and W. line, and I
think I cannot do better than write to the Leaper on
the matter, and bring this latest bit of shuflling respon-
sibility under the notice of its numerous readers. I
am tired of writing about it, and I leave it to you now
whether or not you will settle the thing without any
further talk about the North Western people.”

It will be observed that the bicycle ticket issued by
the English L. and N. W. Railway was to Dublin, The
bicycle was delivered safely at Dublin, and the L. and
N. W. Railway having performed their contract, went
out of the story. The passenger purchased a bicycle
ticket from the Great “ Sick '’ Sourface Railway of Tre-
land for the carriage of his bicycle to Cork. The Great
“Sick ”* Railway did not perform its contract properly,
and the passenger applied for 6s. expenses consequent
on failure to perform the contract satisfactorily. The
letter from which we have quoted was written on Sep-
tember 13th. Previously on the 23rd August Mr. J.
H. Bell, Superintendent of the Great “ Sick ”’ and Sour-
face line, wrote that he had “ the matter in hands with
the Irish Manager- of the L. and N. W. Railway, and
as soon as I hear from that gentleman, I will give you a
definite reply.”” On the 7th September, Mr. J. H.
Bell, replying to a post-card from the passenger, wrote—
“TI have the matter in hands with the L. and N. W.
Company, but have not heard definitely from them yet.
This company is not in any way to blame for the alleged
delay to your bicycle, and, therefsre, I must refer you
to the contracting carriers. Meanwhile I am pressing
the N. W. Company for a definite reply.”” On the 13th
September the passenger sent Mr. Bell the logical and
calm letter which we quoted first, and in which he inci-
dentally said :—“T think I cannot do better than write
to the LEADER on the matter, and bring this latest bit
of shuffling responsibility under the notice of its numer-
ous readers.”’ Hot-foot on receipt of this Mr. J. H.
Bell replied sending the passenger the six shillings he
claimed! On the 15th September Mr. Bell, in reply to
the passenger’s letter of the 13th, wrote:—“I am in
receipt of your letter of 13th inst., and beg to inform
you that the staff of this company do not perform any
duties at the London and N. W. platform at North
Wall. However, as a special case, and without pre-
judice, T enclose Postal Order for 6s., and shall thank
you to acknowledge same on accompanying form.”’

And that is ‘the end of the first short story of the
Great “Sick’’ and Sourface Railway. The second
short story is more interesting, as there is a lady as well
as a bicycle in the case. We will publish it next week.

As our readers know the first edition of “ The Philo-
sophy of Irish Treland’” was bought up with unexpected
rapidity. The printers unfortunately broke up the
type.  They did not anticipate a call for a second
edition ; indeed some “ experts '’ shook their heads du-
biously at the prospects awaiting the first. Tho book
and the type having gone, orders still came from the
trade. There i3 sugh evidence of strong demand for

the hook that, though it entails the reprinting of it,
We cannot say

we have ordered a second edition.

THE LEADER. R o

writing to me to the effect that the matter is being taken
up by you with the North Western people, as I fail en-
tirely to see how they are the contracting parties. I

oxactly the date upon which it will be ready, for
printers’ promises are not infallible guides; but we will
push on the publication of it as quickly as possible.

We unintentionally did Brother Goulding, “ Gentle-
man,’”’ an injustice last week. We incidentally re-
minded our roaders that he was Inner Guard Sword-
Bearer of the Secret Society, the Freemasons of Ireland.
In that statement we erred. Brother Goulding is on
the climb in the Secret Society; he was Inner Guard
Sword-Bearer, but, unlike the Ordinary Shares of the
Great Sourface Railway, he has recently risen. At
the time of the last general meeting of the Great Sour-
face Railway (in August last) Great Sourface Ordinary
Shares were a fraction over 92; as we
write they are toppled down to 854, and
even ab that are, as the stock-jobbers’
lingo has it, “rather sellers.”” A few months ago Bro-
ther Goulding, “ Gentleman,’” wielded the Inner Guard
Sword ; since then he has thrown down the sanguinary
weapon, and though the poor shareholders of the Great
Sowrface Railway may be ‘““sick,’”’ the “ gentleman ’’ is
buoyant, and has climbed up to the position of *“ Grand.
Steward ”’ of the Secret Society.

From an editorial article in the lamb-like Belfast
News Letter, we learn that—* The working of the Local
Government Act by the Nationalists has proved that
they have no conception of justice, and that whercver
they have power those who differ from them in politics
or religion will be oppressed and persecuted.’”

Here is a breezy bit of Orange political philosophy:
from the same lamb-like organ:—‘There can be no:
such thing as a Unionist-Nationalist ;it is a contradic-
tion in terms. A nation is a community which has poli-
tical independence, and the development of Irish na-
tionality is the development of Irish independence. But
Ireland is not a nation, and Unionists are determined
that it never shall be a nation—as fully determined as
the Northern States of America were with regard to the
Southern States. This is a matter on which compro-
mise is as impossible as it was between President Lincoln
and Jefferson Davis. The controversy can only end hy
one side yielding to the other, and Unionists do not in-
tend to yield. The Nationalists must, sooner or later,.
accept the Unicn as an accomplished fact, and accom-
plished for all time.””

To those who do not like our generalisation that
roughly the non-Catholics are England’s Faithful Gar-
rison in Ireland, we commend the following from such
a high authority on the Protestant side as the Belfast
News Letter :—“ Mr. Talbot-Crosbie denies that the:
Protestants of Ireland are opposed to a national policy.
He might as well deny his own existence. He has no
more right than Archbishop Walsh or Sir Antery Mac-
Donnell to speak for Irish Protestants, and we hope
that for the future he will not try to mislead the British
people by doing so.”

The cuwrrent number of the New Ireland Review opens
with an interesting article entitled “ The Outlanders of’
Ulster,”” by Mr. Arthur Synan, advocating that the
North-East corner of Ulster should be given over to
Britain, or to itself, and that in any scheme of Home
Rule the rest of Ireland alone should be involved. The:
article is interestingly written, and, speaking gencrally,
has our full sympathy. Mr, Joseph P. Fallon contri-
butes under the title of “ From Dublin to Alaska,” a
chapter of experiences of “roughing it in the far
Novth-West that may prove wholesome reading both for
the young man, suffering from the emigration fever, and'
for the young gentleman who thinks it beneath him and:
a hardship to commence life any lower than in a soft: job
in the Dublin Corporabion or other public Board. The
picture which Mr. Fallon draws of his unscrupulous
partner whilst he was in the “ real estate *’ line throws a
vivid light on some Yankee methods of business; and his
graphic accounts of the hard life in Alaska shows gold-
hunting in anything but a mellow light. Editing the

LEADER is luxury in comparison. By the way, Mr.
Joseph P. Fallon is a brother of Mr. Fallon of Fallon
and Co. the well-known school publishers, and we under-
stand that he has now joined that firm. Amongst the
other contributors to the October number of the re-
view are Mr. Arthur Clery, K.C., Rev. George O’Neill,
S.J., and Mr. A. E. Clery, LL.B.

In sending us a copy of a letter which he addressed
to the paper concerned, Mr. R. Gibson, Salesmaster,
Public Creamery Market, Limerick, writes:—*Dear
Sir,—A paper called 7he Irish Prolestant, with that
grand spirit of liberality and tolerance, for which a
§mall section of Irish Protestants are so notorious, ob-
jects to my expressing my estcem and admiration for
the Rev. Dr. O’Riordan’s work in Limerick, and for
his book “ Catholicity and Progress.”” Perhaps you
can find room to publish my reply. Every Protestant
whom I have met, who has read Dr. O’Riordan’s book
agrees with me in saying that it is as fine a bit of work
as anyone need wish to see. I say that every man,
woman, and grown child in the country ought to read,
and read carefully, that work. It contains the truth
and nothing but the truth, but yet not the whole truth,
for Dr. O’Riordan by no means makes the picture so
black as we who have studied Irish history know it to
be. Out of regard for his readers’ feelings he has
spared us many a blow that he could in all truth have
given us Protestants.—Yours truly, “R. Grson.”

The letter, a copy of which Mr. Gibson encloses, has
already been published in the daily press. Whether our
funny but not'over prosperous contemporary, edited by
the Grand Master of the Independent Orangemen, pub-
lished it or not we do not know.

On Wednesday evening, October 11th, Father Gay-
nor C.M., of Cork, will lecture in the Dun, Queen’s
Street, Cork. The lecture is entitled “Dangers to
Irish Music in the Irish Revival,”” and it will be de-
livered under the auspices of the Coipoe Ceanncaip
of Cork. The lecture is likely to excite discussion as
well as interest for Father Gaynor has views of his
own on Irish music. We hope that all our Cork readers
who take an interest in Irish music will attend the
lecture.

A vacancy exists for a superintendent of the nursing
staff at the Waterford Infirmary in consequence of a
resignation. . The vacancy was advertised in several
medical and other journals, and in response so large was
the number of applications received that a sub-committee
was appointed to deal with them. The sub-committee
comprised four members, two of whom we understand
are gritty “saved,” and the other two rather “tame’”
“Tdolators.”” It is said that the number of applicants
for the post reached a total of 103 ; be that as it may,
the number was very large, and of the three selected
for the final test all are “saved.” What were the two
“tames ’’ doing? Surely there was at least one compe-
tent “Idolatrous’’ applicant amongst the total number
fis - to go before the Committee at the final election.
The election takes place to-morrow (Friday), and we
hope that. this selection of three “Saved’’ to the ex-
clusion of all “Idolators’’ will be questioned and a

" - demand made for the reason why.

Why do mot the Cork tobacconists support the Irish
tobacco firms better than they do. A visitor to Cork
writes us:—“ We are all aware of the existence in Cork,
of a very vigorous branch of the Industrial Association,
and also of its efforts to create an intelligent and prac-
tical interest in fayour of Irish manufactures generally.

- Is it not regrettable then to find some of the principal

tobacconists’ windows in the city completely dressed ’
with goods of the English Tobacco Combine—Goodbody’s
Gallaher’s, etc., being entirely excluded? If you
should consider this worth publishing, I hope it will
have the effect of the Cork tobacconists above mentioned
giving at least an equal ‘show’ in their windows to
Irish manufactured tobaccos in fubure.’”

The importations of British post office officials into
this country continues. The latest, or anyway, one of
the latest, importations is that of one D. MacPhail, ex-
Postmaster, Laurencekirk, Stirlingshire, Scotland—
where the “experts’’ come from—to be Postmaster of
Roscommon. We understand that Sandy’s post at
Laurencekirk was worth £110 a year; the Postmaster-
ship of Roscommon, into which this Scotchman has been
“ dumped,”’ is worth £150. It is forty pounds increase
for Sandy, but what of the officials of the Irish service?

This year’s Conference of the Catholic Truth Society,
which opens on Wednesday next, October 11th, when
the Bishop of Ross, Most Rev. Dr. Kelly, will deliver
an address, “ The Practical Application of Christianity
to the Lives of the Irish People of To-day,”’ promises
to be an unusually interesting one. Dr. Kelly’s open-
ing address will be delivered in the Round Room, Man-
sion House, and the proceedings will commence at 8
o’clock in the evening. The Cardinal will preside, and
amongst, the speakers will be his Grace of Tuam. The
Conference will be continued during the morning and
afternoon of the following day Thursday. At the morn-
ing sitting Father Halpin, P.P. of Scariff will read a
paper on “ Temperance in Relation to National Pros-
perity,”” and Mr. Charles Dawson will address the Con-
ference on “ One Remedy for Emigration.”” At the
evening sitting Father T. A. Finlay, S.J., whose name
has recently been so mixed up with the University
problem, will turn his attention to “Primary Educa-
tion,”” and Dr. J. R. O’Connell, Solr., will discuss
“ Municipal Libraries.””  On Thursday evening the
President of the Society, His Grace of Tuam, will hold
a reception at the Gresham Hotel.

THE DROGHEDA SHOW.

ROGHEDA' is an interesting town; it is old
enough to be quaint, but withal it is, to the eye
of a visitor, a prosperous-looking town as towns go in
Ireland. In certain features nearly all Irish towns are
similar, and wherever you go you see the little thatched
houses with the little window showing a few clay pipes,
some apples, a bar of soap, and a few ha’penny lucky
bags. We only noticed one shop with an Irish name
over the door, and that shop was of such a character as
to come within call of Daniel Tallon’s Magic Button.
The principal thoroughfare is a prosperous-looking one,
and it has some fine shops. Several drunkeries in the
town have happily  gone broke;’’ and if a few more of
them followed suit, it would be a great advantage to
Drogheda. There is not very much industry left in the
town ; the shipping would give you the shivers, and the
comparative proximity to Dublin diverts a good deal of
what otherwise would be local business to the metro-
polis. Ladies’ dress materials are extensively made in
Drogheda, and the whole output is, we understand, dis-
posed of—in England. Several hundreds of hands are
cmployed in this industry. The pay for the girl
workers, however, is poor. The girls who do the hard
work for the small pay are probably exclusively “ Idola-
tors;’’ all, or nearly all, the holders of good positions
are “saved.”” There is a brewery run by the C. B.
and Drogheda Breweries Co. in the town also, and an
artificial manure works—in these two cases that class of
“free play of competition;”’ such as operates through- -
out the service of the G. N. Railway, sends the “saved’’ .
into tho better jobs also; ¢ Idolators’’ are. far better,
* on the face of it, for coming first out of such things as
Tntermediate competitions than.they are for rising to
* _decont posts in concerns engaged in -the exalted work -
of making beer and mixing artificial manures. Par-
haps if a few men of the Mr. Sullivan stamp overhauled
a few of these places in Drogheda the inexorable law of

bl_le surviva} of the fittest might work cut somewhat
differently in future. However, we only refer to this
phase of life in Drogheda in a passing way ‘at present ;
there are other places besides Belfast into ‘which ‘we
may, at some future time, take occasion to inquire at
some length.

Of course, the Show was chiefly of local interest.
Ducks from one side of the town competed with ducks
fx:cm the other; huge turkeys tried conclusions in the
big grade, and graceful pigeons in tho little grade.
Dogs of all kinds, ugly, big, slender, and tall; trusses
of bay, bunches of flowers, plants, potatoes, carrots,
oats, barley, and so on vied with one another in their
own spheres; and we feel sure that not one of the
judges in all these competitions is now universally
popular.  Useful compefitions in home-made jam,
brown and white bread, sweet cakes, hen eggs, honey,
home-cured bacon and ham, butter, laun ry work,
needlework, hand-knitting, darning, crochet, lace,
home-made art work, ete., ind::ate with what thorough-
ness the promoters of the Show set themselves to stimu-
iate and encourage the various useful activities that
naed all the encouragement they can get in Ireland. A
unique competition, but one which we hope will be on
the programme of every such Show in future, was that
in which a prize was awarded to the trader who dealt
in t e largest amount of Irish-manufactured goods.

It was the third and biggest Show yet; and men like
Dr. Bradley, who put their hearts into the work, have

the satisfaction of knowing that a considerable effect has

resulted from all the trouble and worry incidental to
building up this Show as an annual feature of Drogheda
life.

The Glencree Boys’ Band supplied the music, and the
programme wound up with “A Nation Once Again.”
There were no foreign pedlars plying their trade as at
the Royal Dublin Society’s Horse Show, but there was
a representative selection of Trish manufacturers dis-
playing their goods on the grounds Pierce’s, of Wex-
ford, had a display there. Carroll’s, of Dundalk, had
some machinery set up, and the public could witn
snuff being made, and their well-known mixture, “Tra-
veller’s Joy,”’ being dried. By the way, Messrs. Car-
roll are about starting out in the cigarette-making
branch of the tobacco industry. Boot creams, boot
and furniture polishes, etc., were represented by the two
now well-known firms, Malone Bros., of Great Strand
St., Dublin, and M‘Menamin Bros., of Rathfarnham.
The entire stock of each was purchased by two local
firms. Mr. M. J. Hevern, the representative of
Messrs. Malone Bros., disposed of the entire contents
of his stall to Mr, J. J. Curran, of Drogheda, and Mr.
Joseph M‘Menamin disposed of his firm’s exhibits to
Messrs. M‘Quillan’s, Ltd., of Drogheda. By the way,
the latter distributing firm had a splendid exhibit of
their own composed entirely of goods- of Irish manufac-
ture—Irish cigarettes, soap, Murphy’s-starch, candles,
biscuits, etc., etc. If distributing firms throughout
Ireland would display the spirit manifested by this
exhibition of Messrs. M‘Quillan’s, a new complexion
would soon be seen on the face of industrial Ireland.
Before we leave boot polish, let us comment on a grave
and interesting matter—Whether the over-worked
policemen of this county display more concern in play-
ing concertinas or in putting a shine on their boots,
would be a difficult problem to solve. It appears that
the policeman is rather a conservative in the matter of

_his brogue shiners. Mr. Bobby does not-take kindly te

a new thing—even though an Irish thing—in blacking,

and in Ballyhob, if the bobby is not a judge of black-

ing, who is? Indeed, to a large extent, Mr. Bobby is

a leader of fashion in the matter of boot polish. Per-

haps one of the Irish firms would invent a competition

calculated to break Mr. Bobby’s statuesque square
round John Bull’s blacking. An offer of a concertina
and a fishing-rod to the over-worked R.I.C. man who
used most Irish boot polish might produce an effect.

To return to the Show, the firm of O’Reilly. of Poolbeg

8t., the well-known Irish hox-makers, displayed a selec-

tion of their work, and Messrs. A.-and J. O'Reilly were

on the spot looking after the interests of ‘their firm.
~ We also noticed a fine display of proserved fruit-made at
~ Drogheda, and of Navan bacon and hams.

THE NATIONAL BOARD AND THE PROPOSED
AMALGAMATION OF SCHCOLS.

NSTINCTIVELY, and by the guidance of their
ceclesiastical authorities, Catholics have always
been in favour of ““separate ” schools for boys and girls.
Experience confirms the wisdom of this procedure. The
Catholics of Treland are the most moral people in the
world.  The recently published Intermediate returns
show that under anything like equal conditions Catholic
Intermediate Schools can more than hold their own. Of
Primary Schools, those of the Christian Brothers—
though wholly unendowed—hold the very first place;
and of the schools controlled by the “ National ”’ Board,
none rank as high as the 298 Convent Schools.  Cir-
cumstances sometimes render it impossible to have
“separate ”’ schools. A small island or a remote and
sparsely inhabited locality must be satisfied with the
same school for boys and girls—" a mixed school.”” But
the ideal towards which Catholics look is one school for
boys and another school for girls.

The recently enacted rules—1275, 186, and 187—of
the National Board, if carried out, will ultimately
change a large number of Catholic “ separate '’ schools
into “ mixed ’’ schools; and such “ mixed *’ schools will,
in most cases, be taught by female teachers.

The Commissioners may have deceived themselves,
and they certainly are trying hard to deceive the public
by taking a partial view of the case. They dwell
largely on the advantages of having children of tender
years—4, 5, 6, and 7 years—taught by female teachers.
We are willing to admit that for such children a well-
taught “infants’ school,’”” or a well-managed “infants’
department,’” such as may be found in most of our Con-
vents, is the best possible place. But when a boy is
8, 9, or 10, and far more so when he is 11, 12, 13, or
14, the proper place for him is a well-managed boys’
school. As a rule, female teachers cannot well manage
grown boys; and there are several subjects such as
Geometry, Algebra, Mensuration, Book-keeping, which
are rarely taught by ordinary female teachers.

The new rules will inevitably reduce, especially in
rural places, the number of boys’ schools.  Boys in
such localities must spend all the years of their school-
life—the years on which their success in the world will
depend—in schools taught by mistresses.  Education-
ally, this is a great mistake. The fact that the school
in question—one taught by a mistress—happened to
suit a pupil when 5, 6, or 7, is little compensation to
him for the fact that it does not suit him when he is
12, 13, or 14. In tho world a boy has to struggle for
oxistence, and the best preparation for this struggle is
to be found in a well-conducted boys’ school.

The difficulty with regird to the proper employment
of children of 5, 6, and 7 years cah be met by means
whic¢h will not ‘ecessarily lead—as Rule 1275, 186, 187
will lead-=ito‘the abolition of a number of boys’ schools.

Educdtionally, ‘therefore, the new rules are a grave
misteko.

' Tho Commissioners, in their Report for 1904, “ pooh-
pooh ’’ il idea that the “ co-education of the sexes is
franght with ;grave moral danger.”  They quote Mr.
Daly, who says that as far as he knows * English expe-
rience in rural districts does not lend any colour *’ to
the view that “ mixed schools may be a possible danger

%o mora,l’i’ty ;" and they quote the “ Chief Inspectors in
Ireland »* w])x)o state that “no abuse ever came under
their notice. On the other hand Catholic ecclesiasti-
«cal authorities are unanimous in objecting, except where
unavoidable, t? mixed schools; and notwithstanding the
Commllss.mnors stzytemc_nts, Catholic parents will prefer
the opinion of their priests and bishops. Besides every
Cz\.thol_lc parent knows that if at all possible he would
not wish to send his girls of 13, 14, or 15 to schools
attended by boys of the same age. In such matters, at
-all events, Ireland does not look on England and Scot-
land as models for imitation.

The new rules, while pressing severcly on Catholic
schools, are so framed as to leave a loop-hole for Protes-
tant schools.  In the case of “separate or ordinary
:schools for boys and girls adjoining, or in close proxi-
mity,”” in other words, in the case of Catholic schools,
amalgamation must take place on the retirement of a
principal if either school has dropped below 30. In the
case of Protestant small schools, and there are hun-
dreds of unnecessary small Protestant schools in the
North of Ireland, the attendance must fall below an
average of 25 before official action is taken; and even
then, the Commissioners, far from saying that amalga-
mation must take place, merely indicate that they will
“ consider a re-arrangement of schools in the district.”

It is plain, therefore, that while small Catholic
schools, which are generally “separate schools in ad-
joining buildings, must be amalgamated. Small Pro-
testant schools have a good chance of being allowed to
continue as at present.

The Treasury, in the published correspondence,
clearly indicates that it aims at effecting a saving by
the amalgamation of schools. On the other hand, the
oners and their officers are loud in asserting
nomy *’ has had nothing to do with the intro-
of the new rules. We have no doubt that the
Treasury wished to effect a saving, but in Ireland the
Treasury seldom acts without being impelled by more
than one motive. For example, the Treasury wished to
save the £12,000 so usefully spent on Irish, but the
"Treasury was more actuated by the wish to check the
growth of Irish than by the wish to save the money.
‘If the Treasury merely wished to save money, it has
before it, year after year, the means of doing so. Year
after year the Commissioners, with the approval of the
“Treasury,, insist on spending over £30,000 on a class of
schools now wholly unnecessary—the Model Schools.
For many years these costly failures were defended on
the ground that they trained up young teachers; but
the creation of the Training Colleges has removed this
plea. So far from being willing to economise by
:abolishing these unnecessary Model Schools which cost
£30,374 18s. 8d. during the year 1904, the Treasury
-and the Commissioners have “been pleased ' to allo-
.cate a further sum of £1,790 for future years. This,
however, has been done so skilfully that it has hitherto
attracted little notice.

The truth is that the National Board is completely
-out of touch with the Irish people. The Board is the
-creation of a hostile government, and the Trinity Col-
lege faction which dominates the Board is as anti-Irish
‘as it can be. Several of the Catholic Commissioners
were Trinity College students, and so are utterly unfit
“to represent Catholic interests. The few reliable Catho-
lics on the Board can best serve Catholic interests by re-

““tifing from a position in which they have no real in-

fluence. A ‘“National”’ Board which merely repre-
-sents Trinity College must be re-modelled. S.

My correspondent says:—“T don’t think there is any

There are still

I, so far, agree with my correspondent as to allow
that there is no great want of hsstorical writing about
Ircland, but if it is a question of good historical writ-
ing, I fear T must differ. We have plenty of historical
writing which is, so far, good as it can be made by

the people, especially of the young.

honesty, and fairly extensive investigation. ~ When,
however, we come to style, literary form, judiciality (to
make a word), historical perspective and philosophic
vision, T fear we are not so well provided. Have we, for
instarices, any primer of Irish history of the same size and
merit as, say, the late Charles Allan Fyffe’s primer of
Greek history, in Macmillan’s series? If so, who has
written it? What little book have we on Irish history
good enough for the rising race of students? I know
of none which at once fills the need, and rises to the dig-
nity of history. I am fully aware that much has been
written on the history of Ireland as a whole, and on
detached periods, epochs, phases, and episodes of Irish
history. These monographs on detached periods are,
however, only fit for the advanced, and, indeed, the
leisured, student; the young Intermediate, or even
Royal University, student, can hardly be expected to
make them all up, for “exams.”” won't wait, and the
young student must hurry up if he wants to “ get there’”
in time. “What is wanted,”’ says my correspondent,
“is some force which will bring them into the hands of
the people, especially of the young people.””  What
the young people want is a book, not books, on Irish
history, and a concise book, too. Have they got any-
thing adequate to their need, at once trustworthy and
interesting? I do not think so.

Moreover, I maintain that not only have we no really
excellent students’ manual of Irish history, but that we
have no excellent history at all. Some speak well of
Father D’Alton’s work, and think it shows literary
finish; for my part I should say that it is judicious and
careful, but not attractive. The young reader will
never lay down the late A. M. Sullivan’s “ Story of
Ireland ”’ to take up the D’Alton History. Personally,
I should say they would be wrong, but I should say it
reluctantly, for the Sullivan book is readable, and at-
tractive to young folk; Father D’Alton, on the other
hand, writes with a sort of temperate dulness which will
speedily dispel the enthusiasm of the most ardent
reader. We want somebody who will write a Short His-

little book “ Cainnc ’’ embodies not only the results

sults of his own study of the subject of language teach-
ing in general. In my opinion its publication will mark
the beginning of a new era in the teaching methods of

the Gaelic League.

We all remember, or rather have come to be able to
recognise, what ainala¢ material the Gaelic League had
to go on with ten years ago to teach Irish. It had no
oxperts from Scotland to draw upon, nor money from
Government. It had, in a way, to make something out
of nothing. Things are better now, but our methods
are still far from perfect. This book of Mr. O’Keane’s
is, however, evidence that how imperfect soever we may
still be as teachers, we at least now appreciate the big-
ness of the task we are engaged in. It also proves that
there are some amongst us who know the best way to
go to work with the material we have to hand. To the
optimist and to the pessimist in the Gaelic League, the
book will come as a sedative. To the workaday student
of the language I make bold to say that it-will come as
a call to lessons from a stand-no-nonsense teacher.

T could wish to quote something from the book itself
here. I find myself in this difficulty, however, that, it
being a class-book, is so exactly that and nothing else,
that you must have the class, and the teacher, and the
blackboard before you can make much use of it. The
author’s aim is to make his class speak Irish from the
start, and right on' to the end, to speak as much Irish
as they can either read or write. Those who were pre-
sent ab this year’s session of the Munster Training Col-
lege know how well qualified Mr. O’Keane is to give

" directions to that end. This is what he does in his
book—gives the directions and gives the Cainnc.

As T have said, I find it difficult to give an idea of
‘the contents of the book. Put briefly it is as nearly
as possible a reproduction in forty-eight lessons of the
earliest words and sentences in Irish a learner in the
ordinary course finds necessary, or rather convenient
to use. It starts with the simplest salutations and
replies and leads on, one lesson per class night, till the
learner is perfect master of a fair amount of everyday,
conversational Irish. Let there be no misgiving about
the matter—if the author’s directions are followed, the
pupil will speak freely all the Irish there is in the book
by the time he is through it. ;

THE IRISH SCORN OF ENGLISH LITERATUREF.

WILL not admit that any man in Ireland to-day is

more anxious for the triumph of Irish Ireland than
1 am. And yet it grieves me to calmly consider that
throughout the length and breadth of Ireland at this
moment, even among our most intellectual men and
women, there is to be found a deep-seated scorn of Eng-
lish literature. Even our promising poets and littera-
teurs, and perhaps they more than all others, appear
to be smitten with this contempt. I refer to Irish Ire-
landers, not the English themselves, not to those writers
who are more English than the English themselves.
Those budding literary Irish Irelanders do not read
Shakespeare, nor Carlyle, nor Browning, nor Emerson,
nor Shelley. They do not read Aristotle, nor Socrates,
nor Mazzini, nor Rousseau, nor any of the master-minds
of the world, although the works of these master-minds
can now be procured in English translations at a merely
nominal price. And while English literature is banned,
we appear to be striving to gratify our growing, but as
yet vague, literary and artistic aspirations by delving,
on the one hand, into the formless mass of ancient Irish
manuscripts, and on the other hand by studying and
devouring the almost equally formless Anglo-Irish pro-
ducts which are, in the main, the outcome of a more
or less shallow acquaintance with the said ancient Irish
manuscripts.

The object of this essay is to suggest whither this
state of affairs is likely to lead us, to raise the question
whether it is a desivable state of affairs, and to indicate
the advantages which a study of English literature is
likely to afford us at this stage of our progress towards
an unvanquishable Irish Ireland.

In the first place, I desire to suggest that the perusab
and study and the translation of the ancient Irish
manuscripts—which are admittedly formless from a
literary point of view, which are not literature but:
morely tho materials from which literature may be.
formed, which are by common consent unmined gold
only—{ desire to suggest that if this course alone and:
of itself be pursued, we are likely to be deluged, not:
with fine modern literary masterpieces, either Irish or-
Anglo-Irish, but we shall have instead a crude, semi-
barbaric, and formless conglomeration of works which
will in no sense bear the impress of literature, but:
which will bear the indelible mark of their incoherent
and unliterary originals.

This would undoubtedly be a deplorable outcome of*
hard and conscientious labour. T therefore submit that.
one of the chief needs of Ivish Ireland to-day is a body;

106

of ardent literary and philosophical students who will
acquaint themselves with the best, the most finished
‘products of the world’s literature, and who will bring
to bear the culbure so obtained upon the incomparable
mass of sterling gold which, beyond the shadow of a
doubt, lies buried in the old Trish manuscripts awaiting
the‘ Shakespeares, the Maeterlincks, the Ibsens, the Tol-
'sg,ol.s, the Shelleys, the Emersons, the Carlyles, the Maz-
2inis who will put it through the crucibles and alembics
of their genius and give to Irish Ireland a modern
literature uovivalled by any in the world.

No one at ti:s tute hour will deny that the rough
material is there; but I contend that it is only the
Tough material and not by any stretch of the imagina-
tion the refined and finished article. Now, in English
literature we have readily accessible the refined gold of
the whole world’s literary form, thought and philosophy.
Why should not Trish litterateurs turn that gold to
account for the benefit of their own native literature?
I see no reason why they should not. Although there
are many things in which Irishmen may not wish to
follow the lead of Englishmen, I see no valid argument
against their doing so in the matter under discussion.

And how has the great literature of England been
built up? Simply by incorporation and assimilation of
the best literary products and precedents that the world
“afforded.

What would Chaucer have given England if he had
not the Greeks and the Frenchmen before him? How
many of the immortal works of Shakespeare could the
English nation boast of if his genius had not been set
to work, even though indirectly, by the literature of
Greece, of Rome, of Italy, of Denmark? Where would
Swinburne be but for classical precedents? If Carlyle
had not delved deeply in German literature, what would
he have given England? And so on, ad nfinitum.
And why should not we follow the example which has
brought to England such a wealth of literary achieve-
ment? In a word, I hold that our countrymen will all
the sooner realise their highest aspirations by bringing
to the task of. upbuilding an immortal Irish literature
an intimate study of the methods and thoughts which
have been productive in England of marvellous results.

Lours H. Vicrory.

7 HATEVER I once was, I am not now a keen
politician ; nevertheless, I cannot even affect
a complete indifference to the political side of the Na-
tional Cause. I recognise that the Irish Party ave,
rightly or wrongly, the chosen of the Irish people for
the task of securing Ireland’s national right to make
her own laws. On the political side the cause of Home
Rule is above all others. It transcends all other poli-
tical causes, even if, in the order of possibility of accom-
plishment, it does not come before them. ITome Rule
is, indubitably, the supreme raison @’étre of the Irish
Party, though minor benefits must, of course, be fought
for as opportunity serves. They go to Westminster
primarily to voice Ireland’s demand for it, and it is by
their success or otherwise in the furtherance of that
.demand that they may expect to be judged.  Not
wholly so, of course. There are other items in the na-
“tional political programme, and the Irish Party are sent
to voice our claim to these. They have done well if,
and in so far as, they have secured remedial legislation
‘in any direction, faulty and inadequate though much of
it may be. But Home Rule, the cause of Ireland’s
‘legislative freedom, is the great plank in their plat-
form on which we suppose them to take their stand.

look for light and guidance on the great question—
How does the Cause of Home Rule stand?

Some well-intentioned people cannot get it out of

poople are expected to learn all kinds of science and cul-

members especially. The sooner this bogey is got rid
of the better. There is also, as a rule, far too much
stiffness and formality. This I hate with all my heart.
If I were manager of a public hall or any such institu-
tion my idea would be to make it not a school of deport-
ment, not an academy of learning, but a genial place
of meeting where all were equal, and each one happier
and more at his ease than he could reasonably hope to
be in any other place in the town.

But there would be simple anarchy. Not a bit of it.
There would be rational liberty and enjoyment; and a
few rules wisely enforced would secure sufficient order
and decorum. I appeal for freedom. I appeal for
honest mirth. My one idea is to get my young fellow
in from the street or the tavern, and I am satisfied to
let him amuse himself as he pleases so long as he doesn’t,
make himself a nuisance.

How, then, is he to be coaxed to come and remain?
That is the pressing difficulty, and one which ought not
to be quite beyond solution. It is with the object of
cliciting views and suggestions from other quarters that
I write this. The winter is coming on. The question
I have asked will probably present itself to many minds,
and few I think will deny its importance.

A large and very representative meeting of the people
of Co. Louth was held in Dundalk on Monday, 23rd
September, to found a Co. Louth University Scholar-
ship in connection with the new scholarship scheme.
Mr. T. J. Byrne, Vice-Chairman Co. Council, tock the
chair, and there were present a great many of the clergy.
members of the Co. Council, District Council, and resi-
dents from North and South of the county. Mr. W.
O'Reilly. D.L., Co. Council, introduced the county
scheme, of which he was the author, and for which such
spontaneous and earnest support has been shown. The
endowment of a scholarship each year for three years te
be held by a County Louth boy at University College,
Dublin, was decided upon, and a committee appointed
to carry out the details. On the motion of Very Rev.
Archdeacon Segrave, P.P., Drogheda, a guarantee fund.
was opened, and £70 subscribed on the spot. It was
resolved on the proposal of Father Quinn, Adm., Pre-
sident Dundalk Branch Gaelic League, supported by
Mr. O’Meara, Co. Council, Omeath, and Father M.
Finegan, President Drogheda Branch Gaelic League,
to make Irish an obligatory subject in the qualifying
examinations, and the I. and II. Arts course of the
winner of the scholarship.
